script:
export const meta = {
  name: 'glamdring-feature-ideation',
  description: 'Panel of design lenses proposes interesting new features for the Glamdring reaction-combat game, each self-checked for feasibility against the real codebase',
  phases: [
    { title: 'Ideate', detail: 'one agent per design lens, each reads the repo and proposes a feature package' },
  ],
}

const REPO = String.raw`C:\Users\duran\Documents\godot\glamdring`

const SHARED_BRIEF = `
You are a senior game designer + Godot engineer reviewing an existing small game called "Glamdring".

FIRST, read these files to fully understand the game (use Read):
- ${REPO}\\CLAUDE.md  (project overview + conventions)
- ${REPO}\\gameplay.md  (authoritative rules, FSM, timings)
- ${REPO}\\plan.md  (architecture, tunable constants, layout)
- ${REPO}\\balance.json  (runtime tunables)
- ${REPO}\\fight.gd  (tick loop, swing resolves)
- ${REPO}\\player.gd  (player FSM)
- ${REPO}\\enemy.gd + ${REPO}\\enemy_basic.gd  (enemy FSM)
- ${REPO}\\combatant.gd  (base class)
- ${REPO}\\hud.gd
- ${REPO}\\sprites\\SPRITES.md  (sprite cell layout)

THE GAME: 1v1 reaction combat in Godot 4.6. Fixed 16 ticks/sec timestep. Player (left) is INVINCIBLE in v1; goal is to drop enemy HP to 0. Enemy has an FSM: idle -> threat_weak/threat_strong (telegraph) -> recovery -> idle, with an exhausted side-branch when fatigue hits 0. Player has weak attack, strong attack, block (with an 8-tick "active parry" window that drains enemy fatigue), and dodge. The core loop is "read the telegraph, find the opening": you can only damage the enemy during its recovery/exhausted windows; hitting it while idle does nothing (it blocks), and getting hit REGENERATES the enemy (passivity is punished).

HARD ARCHITECTURE CONSTRAINTS (a proposal that violates these is bad):
- NO physics, NO Area2D, NO CollisionShape — everything is stateful, manual.
- All gameplay logic runs strictly in _on_tick() at 16 Hz. Rendering via queue_redraw().
- Gameplay tunables go in balance.json (loaded by the Balance autoload). Visual/animation constants stay as const in scripts.
- Sprites are a horizontal strip, one cell per FSM stance, swapped via region_rect — NO inter-pose animation, NO AnimationPlayer. Player sheet = 7 cells, enemy_basic = 6 cells. Adding a brand-new visual stance ideally REUSES an existing cell or a modulate tint (the user would have to draw new art otherwise — call this out).
- Changes to rules/timing MUST be sync-able to gameplay.md + plan.md (note this, don't write them).

YOUR LENS: ${'{LENS}'}

Through THIS lens, propose a COHESIVE package of 2-4 new features that would make the game genuinely more interesting/fun WITHOUT breaking the careful existing design. Each feature must be concrete and tied to the real code: name the exact files, FSM states, and balance.json keys you'd add or change, with specific tick timings/values. Prefer features that need no new sprite art (reuse cells / tints / pure fx); if a feature needs art, say so and give an art-free fallback.

Be bold and specific — the user explicitly asked to be surprised. But every idea must be implementable in this tick-based, no-physics, sprite-swap architecture. Self-assess feasibility honestly.`

const SCHEMA = {
  type: 'object',
  additionalProperties: false,
  required: ['packageName', 'theme', 'features', 'cohesionRationale', 'feasibilityScore', 'feasibilityConcerns', 'topPick'],
  properties: {
    packageName: { type: 'string' },
    theme: { type: 'string', description: 'one sentence: the through-line of this package' },
    features: {
      type: 'array',
      items: {
        type: 'object',
        additionalProperties: false,
        required: ['name', 'pitch', 'mechanics', 'filesTouched', 'newStates', 'newBalanceKeys', 'needsNewSpriteArt', 'spriteWorkaround', 'juiceFactor', 'depthFactor', 'effort', 'risks'],
        properties: {
          name: { type: 'string' },
          pitch: { type: 'string', description: '1-2 sentences on why it is fun' },
          mechanics: { type: 'string', description: 'detailed gameplay rules incl. tick timings and resolve order' },
          filesTouched: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' } },
          newStates: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' }, description: 'new FSM states, or empty' },
          newBalanceKeys: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' } },
          needsNewSpriteArt: { type: 'boolean' },
          spriteWorkaround: { type: 'string', description: 'how to do it art-free, or "" if it needs art' },
          juiceFactor: { type: 'integer', description: '1-5, how much it improves game feel' },
          depthFactor: { type: 'integer', description: '1-5, how much strategic depth it adds' },
          effort: { type: 'string', enum: ['S', 'M', 'L'] },
          risks: { type: 'string' },
        },
      },
    },
    cohesionRationale: { type: 'string', description: 'why these features work together' },
    feasibilityScore: { type: 'integer', description: '1-10 honest self-assessment of how cleanly this fits the architecture' },
    feasibilityConcerns: { type: 'string' },
    topPick: { type: 'string', description: 'if only ONE feature from this package could ship, which and why' },
  },
}

const LENSES = [
  { key: 'skill-ceiling', lens: 'SKILL CEILING & MASTERY — features that reward precise timing and reading, give the expert player tools to express skill (perfect-parry/riposte, just-frame inputs, punish windows, stagger/poise). Make the existing "find the opening" loop deeper for someone who has mastered the basics.' },
  { key: 'juice-feel', lens: 'JUICE & GAME FEEL — features that make every hit and moment satisfying within a sprite-swap + draw_rect engine: hit-stop/freeze-frames, screen flash, slow-mo, dynamic camera zoom/shake, afterimages, particles, chromatic punch, sound-cue hooks. Pure feel, minimal new rules, maximal "wow".' },
  { key: 'ai-depth', lens: 'ENEMY AI DEPTH & VARIETY — make the opponent less predictable and more alive: feints/cancels, multi-hit combo strings, adaptive behaviour that reads the player, delayed/charged attacks, unblockable tells, a second enemy archetype (note skeleton-basic.png exists in sprites/). New reads and mind-games for the player.' },
  { key: 'risk-reward', lens: 'RISK / REWARD SYSTEMS & STAKES — meters and resources that create meaningful decisions: a rage/momentum/focus meter that builds and is spent, combo counters with damage scaling, an optional player-HP/lose-condition mode that raises stakes, high-risk-high-reward attack options, "execute" finishers on low-HP enemies.' },
  { key: 'meta-replay', lens: 'REPLAYABILITY & PROGRESSION — reasons to play again: endless waves of escalating enemies, a score/combo-rank system (S/A/B/C), run modifiers/mutators, a duel timer with bonuses, unlockable enemy variety, difficulty ramps. Keep it inside the single fight.tscn scene where possible.' },
]

phase('Ideate')
const proposals = await parallel(LENSES.map(L => () =>
  agent(SHARED_BRIEF.replace('{LENS}', L.lens), {
    label: `ideate:${L.key}`,
    phase: 'Ideate',
    schema: SCHEMA,
    agentType: 'Explore',
  }).then(r => r ? { lens: L.key, ...r } : null)
))

return proposals.filter(Boolean)

result.cohesionRationale: These four features form a **mastery-ladder loop**: punishing stagger chains teaches high-APM combo potential; riposte windows reward precise parry timing; fatigue tiers create dynamic threat assessment per hit; counter-stance gives the opponent intelligent pushback, forcing the expert player to read enemy intent after pressure. Together they deepen the "read the opening" core by making openings narrower, time-critical, and contextual — pure skill expression.
result.feasibilityConcerns: Stagger and counter-stance both add new FSM states, which is manageable but requires careful resolve order (stagger blocks enemy actions, counter-stance must interleave cleanly with threat/recovery). Riposte requires tracking a transient flag across ticks. Fatigue tiers alter AI probabilities and threat/recovery durations dynamically per tick, adding some complexity to enemy._decide_attack(). All four fit the tick-based architecture and need zero sprite art (can reuse cells with tint modulate for stagger/counter visuals). Main risk: balance tuning — these features interact (stagger breaks enemy chains, riposte incentivizes block, counter-stance discourages pure aggression) and if timings overlap badly, the loop becomes frustrating rather than fun.
result.topPick: Riposte Window is the top pick: it's the simplest to implement (one bool flag, one damage multiplier), has the highest juice-to-effort ratio (active parries go from "prevent regen" to "unlock burst damage"), immediately teaches expert players to seek parry setups, and fits the existing active block mechanic like a glove. It's the foundation that works even without the other three.
result.packageName: Skill Ceiling Mastery Suite: Poise, Riposte, Fatigue Tiers, Counter-Stance
result.theme: Narrow the opening: make every hit count, every parry matter, every enemy recovery a fork in the road between punish and counter.

features.name: Stagger on Hit Chain
features.pitch: Landing 2+ weak attacks within 20 ticks (1.25 sec) stuns the enemy briefly, creating a thin window where high-APM players can chain into heavy damage. Rewards rhythm and speed-reading.
features.mechanics: New enemy state `staggered` (6 ticks, entered only from idle/recovery when hit streak >= 2). Track hit_streak counter on enemy (increments on weak hit, resets on tick > 20 since last weak hit or when entering threat/exhausted). On weak swing resolve: if hit_streak reaches 2 and enemy in idle/recovery, `enemy.enter_stagger()` interrupts current state. Staggered state: enemy cannot attack, cannot block, takes full damage. After 6t → back to idle. Visually: enemy cell index 5 (reuse exhausted cell or modulate tint to cyan/blue). Balance keys: `enemy_basic.stagger_ticks := 6, stagger_hit_count_threshold := 2, stagger_hit_streak_reset_ticks := 20`.
features.spriteWorkaround: Reuse exhausted cell (idx 5) with modulate tint `Color(0.5, 0.8, 1.0, 1.0)` (cyan wash) to visually distinguish stagger. No new cell needed.
features.risks: Hit streak counter can feel fiddly if reset timings don't line up; if stagger is too frequent, it trivializes enemy offense. Tuning window (20 ticks = 1.25 sec) is key — too short punishes non-spam players, too long removes urgency. Also: must prevent stagger from triggering during threat phases (only idle/recovery) else telegraph becomes interruptible, breaking core design.

features.name: Riposte Window (Active Parry Bonus)
features.pitch: Successfully executing an active parry (first 8 ticks of block during weak attack) marks the player's next attack as a 'riposte', dealing 1.5x damage. Encourages skilled blocking → counterattack chains.
features.mechanics: Add `riposte_active: bool` flag to player. Set to true when active parry fires (`_resolve_enemy_swing()` calls `enemy.drain_fatigue()` and `player.set_riposte_ready(true)`). On player swing: if `riposte_active` and damage is dealt, multiply damage by 1.5× (weak: 15→22.5, strong: 25→37.5; round to 23/38 or keep float). Immediately set `riposte_active = false` after swing resolve. Visually: during next attack after parry, sprite gets a brightening tint (modulate `Color(1.5, 1.0, 1.0, 1.0)` — hint of gold/red). Balance keys: `riposte_damage_multiplier := 1.5`.
features.spriteWorkaround: No new sprite cell. Apply a brief brightening modulate (1.5 red channel) during the swing state tick when riposte is active, then revert. Pure visual tint, reuses existing stance cells.
features.risks: If multiplier is too high (>1.5×), riposte spam becomes overpowered and trivializes blocking. If too low (<1.3×), players won't feel the reward. Sweet spot likely 1.4–1.5×. No FSM risk since it's just a flag on player. Elegant and low-friction.

features.name: Fatigue Tiers (Dynamic Enemy Behavior)
features.pitch: Enemy's attack patterns and timings shift dynamically based on current fatigue: high fatigue = urgent/longer idle, low fatigue = aggressive shorter telegraph. Creates tension and forces players to adapt playstyle per fight phase.
features.mechanics: In enemy._decide_attack(), check fatigue tier before choosing attack. Define tiers: `high (> 30 fatigue): 60% weak, 40% strong, idle 32–64t; mid (15–30): 70% weak, 30% strong, idle 24–48t; low (<15): 50% weak, 50% strong, idle 16–40t.` Also shorten threat/recovery by ~10% per tier drop (high: normal 24t/32t; mid: 22t/30t; low: 20t/28t). Enemy becomes more desperate/unpredictable as fatigue drains. Visually: no new states, but enemy sprite gets a subtle tint modulate based on tier: high=white, mid=yellow wash (0.9, 0.85, 0.6, 1.0), low=orange/red wash (1.0, 0.6, 0.5, 1.0). Balance keys: `fatigue_tier_high_min, fatigue_tier_mid_min, [weak/strong]_probability_[high/mid/low], [threat/recovery]_ticks_[high/mid/low]`.
features.spriteWorkaround: Apply fatigue-tier color tint to enemy sprite modulate in _update_sprite(): overlay a subtle wash (high=neutral, mid=0.95/0.85/0.6, low=1.0/0.65/0.5) without changing cell. Pure modulate tint, reuses all existing cells.
features.risks: Adds many balance keys (18 new tunables), can overwhelm tuning. Recommend starting with 3–5 key parameters (e.g., just probability shift per tier, keep threat/recovery durations static) and expand if fun. If tiers shift too dramatically, fights feel disjointed; if too subtle, players won't notice. Also: fatigue regeneration during hit needs to be watched — if player drains to low, then regenerates, tier shifts can cause jarring AI mood swings.

features.name: Counter-Stance (Enemy Defensive FSM Branch)
features.pitch: When hit during recovery or idle, enemy briefly enters a 'counter_stance' telegraph (3 ticks) signaling a counterattack. If player doesn't dodge/block, they take a guaranteed hit on recovery. If they do defend, enemy resets to idle. Adds a risk/reward layer to aggression.
features.mechanics: After _resolve_player_swing() hits enemy in idle/recovery, enemy.enter_counter_threat(kind) where kind is weak/strong (randomly chosen, 70% weak). Counter-threat = 3t telegraph into 4t recovery (shorter than normal threat/recovery to not drag fights). If player dodges/blocks during counter-threat, counter resolves as miss/block (standard rules). If hit lands, player takes minor HP drain (5 pts; player still invincible but telegraphs the cost). Counter-threat can chain: if hit again during counter-recovery, enemy can enter counter-threat again. Visually: counter-threat stance cell (reuse threat_weak/threat_strong indices 1/2 with a brief purple modulate tint 0.7, 0.5, 1.0 to signal 'defensive'). Balance keys: `counter_threat_ticks := 3, counter_recovery_ticks := 4, counter_weak_probability := 0.7, player_damage_on_counter_hit := 5`.
features.spriteWorkaround: Reuse threat_weak (idx 1) and threat_strong (idx 2) cells with a purple modulate overlay (0.7, 0.5, 1.0) to signal 'counter' vs 'normal' telegraph. No new cell drawn, only tint. When resolving counter-threat swing, apply damage to player (invincible but HP-bar impact for narrative tension) and flash player sprite like a weak hit.
features.risks: Adds complexity to swing resolve (now three branches: normal threat, counter-threat, player swing). Order matters: must resolve enemy swing before player swing, and counter-threats must not interrupt player attacks mid-swing (only trigger after player swing lands). If counter-threat frequency is too high, it feels oppressive; if too low, players ignore it. Also: counter chains (hitting enemy during counter-recovery → new counter) can spiral and cause tension loops — may need a cooldown (enemy can only counter once per 2–3 cycles) to keep fights flowing.

result.theme: Make threat windows unmissable and recovery windows feel like earned punish windows; reward aggressive, rhythmic play with hit-stop and visual crescendos.

features.name: Threat Pulse — Animated cell tint on threat state
features.pitch: Enemy flashes yellow on threat onset, pulsing for 2 frames to make the telegraph window unmissable and build urgency. Creates a rhythm cue that players learn to read.
features.mechanics: New `balance.json` key `threat_pulse_color` (yellow, RGB 1.0, 0.85, 0.2). On tick boundary when enemy enters `threat_weak` or `threat_strong`, set flag `threat_pulsing` and `threat_pulse_ticks := 4` (2 frames at 16 Hz visible as bright, then fade). Modulate multiplier: `if threat_pulse_ticks > 2: modulate *= threat_pulse_color else modulate = Color.WHITE`. Decrement `threat_pulse_ticks` each tick in `enemy.gd.tick()` before state countdown. Pure visual, zero gameplay impact.
features.spriteWorkaround: Modulate tint only; reuses existing stance cells.
features.risks: Color choice must be legible on sprite art; cyan/magenta might work if yellow clashes.

features.name: Hit-Stop on Strong Hits — Freeze-frame momentum
features.pitch: Strong attacks and parry drains freeze the screen for 3 ticks, making impact feel HEAVY and giving the player a beat to enjoy the damage. Weak hits don't freeze, so weak spam feels snappy by contrast.
features.mechanics: New `balance.json` key `hit_stop_strong_ticks := 3` and `hit_stop_parry_ticks := 2`. In `fight.gd._resolve_enemy_swing()` when active parry triggers, set `game_hit_stop_ticks := hit_stop_parry_ticks`. In `_resolve_player_swing()` when strong hits land, set `game_hit_stop_ticks := hit_stop_strong_ticks`. Add `game_hit_stop_ticks: int = 0` counter in `fight.gd`. In `_on_tick()`, if `game_hit_stop_ticks > 0`, decrement but **skip the rest of the tick loop** (return early so state countdowns don't advance). Rendering still queues, so particles + flash are visible during freeze. Blocks weak attacks from registering during freeze.
features.spriteWorkaround: No art needed; it's a tick-timing freeze.
features.risks: If too long, feels sluggish; too short, unnoticed. 3 ticks = ~188 ms, feels snappy. Must not apply to weak spam or it breaks rhythm.

features.name: Recovery Window Indicator — Enemy flickers when vulnerable
features.pitch: When enemy enters `recovery_weak` or `recovery_strong`, it flickers red for 1 tick, signalling 'now is your moment to strike.' Creates a visual rhythm that rewards reading the telegraph.
features.mechanics: New flag `enemy_recovery_flash: int = 0` (ticks remaining). On tick boundary in `enemy._advance_state_after_expiry()` when transitioning INTO `recovery_weak` or `recovery_strong`, set `enemy_recovery_flash := 1`. In `_update_sprite()`, if `enemy_recovery_flash > 0`, multiply modulate by `Color(1.2, 0.7, 0.7, 1.0)` (reddish tint). Decrement `enemy_recovery_flash` at start of `enemy.tick()`. Pure polish.
features.spriteWorkaround: Modulate tint; no new cells.
features.risks: Red may overlap visually with hit flash if both trigger simultaneously. Solution: check `hit_flash_ticks` and blend, or accept the flash priority.

features.name: Exhausted Decay Pulse — Danger countdown visual
features.pitch: Enemy exhausted state pulses orange->red over its 64-tick duration, creating mounting visual pressure. In the last 8 ticks, it throbs rapidly, signalling 'you must finish this now or lose your advantage.'
features.mechanics: In `enemy.gd.tick()`, if `state == &"exhausted"`: compute pulse phase as a function of `state_ticks_remaining` (0..64). Map 64->0 to a hue/brightness curve: early exhausted is dim orange, final 16 ticks pulse orange-red 2 ticks per cycle, final 8 ticks pulse at 1 tick per cycle. Implement via `exhausted_pulse_intensity: float = 1.0 - (state_ticks_remaining / 64.0)` and `pulse_color := Color.from_hsv(0.05 + exhausted_pulse_intensity * 0.15, 0.8, 0.6 + exhausted_pulse_intensity * 0.4, 1.0)`. Blend: `if state == &"exhausted": modulate *= pulse_color`. No new balance keys; pure visual math.
features.spriteWorkaround: Pure modulate math on existing exhausted cell.
features.risks: Color math may need tuning; HSV mapping is tricky to preview. Recommend implementing and playtesting. Could be simplified to a binary pulse if HSV feels buggy.

result.cohesionRationale: These four features work together to reinforce the core reaction loop: (1) **Threat Pulse** makes the telegraph unmissable, teaching rhythm. (2) **Recovery Flash** marks the punish window so players learn when to strike. (3) **Hit-Stop on Strong** makes successful reads feel SATISFYING (freeze-frame reward), while weak spam stays snappy. (4) **Exhausted Decay Pulse** creates mounting pressure and urgency as the player approaches victory. Together they form a visual language: *bright yellow = read this*, *red flicker = strike now*, *heavy freeze = you won*, *pulsing orange-red = finish the job*. Zero new rules, zero new art, pure feel. Each can be tuned independently via balance.json or modulate math.
result.feasibilityConcerns: Hit-Stop is the most complex: requires early return logic in _on_tick() to skip state countdowns. Must be carefully ordered so swing resolution still fires (resolve before the early return, or trigger hit-stop *after* resolve). Exhausted Decay Pulse HSV math could introduce floating-point jank; recommend clamping and testing. Modulate blending order matters if multiple effects stack (hit flash + threat pulse + recovery flash); current code does not layer modulates, so conflicts must be resolved by priority (hit flash > threat pulse > recovery flash > exhausted pulse). All four features are purely visual with zero gameplay logic changes, so they don't touch FSM, balance mechanics, or damage numbers.
result.topPick: **Threat Pulse** is the top pick because it solves the highest-value problem: making the telegraph window *readable* and *teachable*. New players struggle to see when the enemy is about to attack; a single yellow flash on threat onset fixes that for free. It has the highest juice-to-effort ratio and no risk of breaking the delicate timing. Ship this first, then add Hit-Stop (strongest payoff for victory) and Recovery Flash (smallest but most satisfying). Exhausted Decay can come later as polish.

result.packageName: Predator's Instinct — Enemy AI Depth Package
result.theme: An intelligent, unpredictable enemy that adapts to the player's defense patterns and resource state, turning fatigue into a shared combat pressure point.

features.name: Feint Cancels
features.pitch: Enemy abandons mid-telegraph and snaps back to idle, forcing the player to commit reads. Not every attack is real.
features.mechanics: During threat_weak (ticks 1–6), enemy rolls abort chance: base 10% + (40 - fatigue) * 0.3%. If true, call _abort_threat() to snap back to idle instantly, no swing fires. Strong threats rarely feint (5%) due to commitment cost. Abort is instant — no recovery tick, straight back to idle. Player sees threat cancel as sprite swap and must read intent.
features.spriteWorkaround: Reuses idle cell. Sprite swap back to idle stance provides instant visual feedback of abort.
features.risks: RNG frustration if probability mistuned. Recommend tuning window 8–12% weak feint rate in playtesting. Too often breaks player reads; too rare is invisible.

features.name: Fatigue-Aware Attack Selection
features.pitch: Enemy picks attack strength based on stamina: low fatigue = weak or skip turn; high fatigue = confident strong attacks. Fatigue becomes a shared tempo dial.
features.mechanics: In _pick_attack(), check enemy.fatigue against three tiers (low_threshold=5, mid=15, high=30). Below low: force weak or skip idle (random); mid: reduce strong to 5%; high: boost strong to 25%. Thresholds are tunable. Creates clear resource narrative: player drains fatigue → enemy forced weak; enemy recovers fatigue → enemy dangerous again. No new cells; optional: tint threat_strong cell slightly differently when in 'low-fatigue weak mode' to telegraph desperation (yellow hue).
features.spriteWorkaround: Reuses all cells. Optional: when forced-weak due to low fatigue, apply modulate tint to threat_strong cell to signal 'desperate weak choice' (e.g., slightly desaturated or yellow); threat_weak cell is always used for actual weak attacks.
features.risks: If thresholds are wrong, AI can feel overly passive (low threshold) or overly aggressive (high threshold). Recommend playtesting at 5/15/30 with normal probability 0.15 strong. Tuning window is tight but highly rewarding.

features.name: Combo Strings
features.pitch: After recovery, enemy chains directly into another threat without idle gap. Back-to-back pressure forces active defense, not passive waiting.
features.mechanics: Add combo_count field to enemy_basic.gd. In _advance_state_after_expiry(), when exiting recovery_weak/recovery_strong: check if combo_count < combo_max_length (3) and RNG < combo_probability (0.40). If true, call _decide_attack() directly (skip idle), else reset combo_count to 0 and _enter_idle(). Chain can break early if player hits during recovery (resets count). Max 3 attacks prevents infinite loops. Visually: sprite swap from recovery directly to next threat is the feedback.
features.spriteWorkaround: Reuses recovery and threat cells. On combo trigger, fight.gd can spawn yellow particles for juice (visual-only, no gameplay impact).
features.risks: Chain can feel overwhelming if probability too high or cap too loose. Recommend testing 0.40 probability + cap 3. If feels unfair in playtesting, reduce to 0.30 or cap 2.

features.name: Parry Counter Flag
features.pitch: When player actively parries (weak block in 8-tick window), enemy retaliates with guaranteed strong attack next turn. Punishes predictable defense and rewards defense variety.
features.mechanics: Add parry_blocked_flag: bool to enemy_basic.gd. In _pick_attack(), if flag is set, force &'strong' and reset flag. In fight.gd._resolve_enemy_swing(), detect active parry (kind == weak AND is_active_block()) and call enemy.set_parry_counter() to set the flag. Next turn, enemy's _pick_attack() skips normal RNG and returns strong. Creates mind-game: parry → counter-strong → block strong fails → hit regen → must vary defense. Optional: reduce threat_strong telegraph by 4 ticks on counter to make counter feel more dangerous.
features.spriteWorkaround: Reuses threat_strong cell. Optional: brief yellow flash when counter triggers (modulate tint on enemy sprite for 2 ticks) to telegraph retaliation.
features.risks: If counter feels unavoidable, player feels punished rather than challenged. Mitigation: only trigger on *active* parry (first 8 ticks of block), not passive, so player has read-ahead agency. Also: make counter a guaranteed *strong threat*, not an unblockable hit — player can still block strong (ineffective but safe).

result.cohesionRationale: The four features form a cohesive **tempo and adaptation loop**. Feint + Combo create unpredictability in attack timing (is it real? is it chained?). Fatigue-Aware sets the **difficulty knob** — low fatigue = forgiving, high fatigue = dangerous, making player fatigue drain a core tactical choice. Parry Counter transforms **player defense from passive safety into a commitment with a cost**, encouraging dynamic block/dodge alternation instead of spam. Together, they elevate the enemy from a predictable pattern (weak weak weak strong) into a reactive, learning opponent that reads the player's choices and adapts. Fatigue becomes a **shared resource pressure point** instead of just a bar. All features use the existing FSM and sprite cells, respect the 16 Hz tick grid, and live in balance.json tunables.
result.feasibilityConcerns: The primary risk is **probability tuning**. Each feature adds RNG decision points (feint chance, fatigue thresholds, combo roll, counter availability). If any probability is off, the feature either dominates (feels cheap) or disappears (invisible). Mitigation: each feature is independently toggleable via balance.json; start with conservative values (e.g., 10% feint, 0.30 combo) and increase after playtesting. Secondary: Parry Counter in fight.gd is a one-line addition, but needs careful integration to ensure it only triggers on active parry, not passive block. Third: Combo chains could create feedback-loop scenarios (e.g., player never gets a turn), but hard cap at 3 prevents runaway. Recommendation: implement in order (Fatigue-Aware first — deepest win, lowest complexity), then add Combo, Parry, Feint incrementally so each tuning iteration is isolated.
result.topPick: **Fatigue-Aware Attack Selection (#2).** It is the mechanical core that elevates the entire game. By making enemy attack choice a direct function of fatigue state, it transforms the otherwise cosmetic fatigue bar into a **shared combat currency**. Player now thinks strategically: "do I burn a parry to drain fatigue now, or save it for the next threat?" This is classic fighting-game tension (stamina pressure) imported into a reaction-combat context. It also requires the fewest code lines (rewrite _pick_attack with three if/elif branches) and is fully orthogonal to the other features, so it can ship alone and is immediately testable. Once in, the other three features layer naturally on top (Fatigue state explains why enemy chains combos when strong, why feints trigger when weak, why counters escalate when provoked). Per-line-of-code, deepest mechanical payoff.

result.theme: Transform passivity punishment into active resource management: a momentum meter that builds on hits and defense, gates high-risk attack options, and executes low-HP enemies with devastating finishing moves.

features.name: Momentum Meter (Player Resource)
features.pitch: Player builds momentum (0–40) on successful hits and skilled parries, spending it on risky high-damage attacks. Empty momentum is a visible failure state; full momentum creates a brief window of overwhelming power that forces commitment.
features.mechanics: Momentum is a new counter in player.gd, capped at 40 (new balance key). On swing resolution in fight.gd: weak hit lands → +3 momentum; strong hit lands → +6 momentum; active parry (fatigue drain) → +5 momentum. Momentum decays slowly: −1 per 16 ticks in idle (0.25 sec), resetting if player takes a hit (enemy regeneration event triggers momentum → 0). Momentum is displayed as a third bar in HUD (yellow-orange gradient, same 120px width as HP bar, placed at y_off=−126, between fatigue and the gap to sprites). Visual feedback: momentum bar pulses (+2% scale jitter) when > 20.
features.spriteWorkaround: Momentum bar is drawn directly in enemy.gd._draw() as a simple colored rect strip, no sprite needed.
features.risks: Decay-on-hit can feel punishing if enemy hits are frequent; playtesting will tune the decay rate. Meter visibility is crucial — if players don't see the momentum bar update, they won't engage with it. Recommend large, glowing bar to telegraph the mechanic.

features.name: Momentum Attack: Cleave (Risky, High Damage)
features.pitch: Spend 30+ momentum for a 12-tick windup attack that deals 40 HP damage, but if dodged or the enemy transitions to threat, momentum is WASTED (high-risk commitment). Encourages aggressive prediction during enemy exhausted phases.
features.mechanics: New FSM state in player: `attacking_cleave` (12-tick windup, no recovery — state ends and returns to idle). Swing fires at tick 12 (last tick of windup). Cannot enter this state unless momentum >= 30. On swing resolution: if enemy in recovery_* or exhausted, damage is 40 HP (locked, no scaling). If enemy in threat_* (enemy attack interrupts), no damage and momentum → 0. If enemy in idle (rare, but possible), no damage (enemy blocks, as normal weak/strong rules). Visually: player uses existing idle cell (0) but modulate tint (0.3, 1.0, 0.8) to show 'charged' state, or reuse attacking_strong_recovery cell (3) if not animating during windup. Momentum is deducted on state entry (not on swing — immediately on input).
features.spriteWorkaround: Reuse attacking_strong_recovery cell (index 3) with a bright cyan modulate tint (Color(0.3, 1.0, 0.8, 1.0)) to distinguish from normal attacks. Or render cleave sprite via a simple debug outline rect in fight.gd._draw() (no sprite asset needed).
features.risks: High complexity: windup timing prediction vs. enemy AI is hard. If cleave windows are too small or enemy AI too aggressive, players will rarely land it and feel frustrated. Cooldown + momentum cost combo must gate spam, or the move becomes trivial. Recommend: 20-tick cooldown, momentum cost 30, damage 40 (so weak is 15, strong is 25, cleave is 40 but risky).

features.name: Execute Threshold: Low-HP Finishing Bonus
features.pitch: When enemy HP drops below 25 (a visible threshold), all player attacks deal +50% damage and ignore the fatigue-drain window — bonus rewards aggressive play when prey is nearly dead and punishes hesitation.
features.mechanics: New game rule in fight.gd._resolve_player_swing(): check if enemy.hp <= 25 before applying damage. If true, multiply weak damage by 1.5 (15 → 22–23, round down to 22) and strong damage by 1.5 (25 → 37–38, round down to 37). Additionally, strong attacks drain enemy fatigue even if enemy is in idle (normally strong only drains in idle; execute bonus makes it drain on top of normal idle block). Visually: when execute threshold is active, enemy sprite gets a red tint overlay (modulate *= Color(1.2, 0.7, 0.7)) on every tick while hp <= 25, pulsing at 4 Hz (blink ticks 0–1 full, 2–3 half). This is drawn client-side in enemy.gd._update_sprite() (no new state needed, just conditional tint).
features.spriteWorkaround: Execute threshold is signaled purely via modulate tint (red pulse overlay) on the existing enemy sprite. No new cell needed.
features.risks: Damage scaling is non-intuitive (22 vs. 15 weak); consider rounding to 23–24 for feel. The red pulse may clash with hit flash (both use modulate); need to ensure hit-flash white overrides execute-red for 1 frame clarity. Threshold of 25 / 40 enemy max HP (62.5%) is high — may trivialize late-game. Recommend tuning threshold to 20 (50%) and damage multiplier to 1.3 (conservative) in early balance, then iterate.

features.name: Riposte Window: Block → Counterattack Link
features.pitch: After a successful active parry (first 8 ticks of block), player has 4-tick riposte window to press attack without losing the parry reward. Miss the window and block ends normally. Land a riposte weak attack and gain +2 momentum and apply it as a free weak damage (15 HP) even if enemy is in idle.
features.mechanics: New sub-state flag in player: `riposte_window_ticks` (init 0). On successful active parry (fatigue drain event in fight.gd), set riposte_window_ticks = 4. Each tick in player.tick(), if riposte_window_ticks > 0, decrement. In player.handle_input(), if state == &"blocking" and riposte_window_ticks > 0 and player presses → (weak attack), enter attacking_weak state AND flag the swing as 'riposte' (new internal flag `is_riposte_attack := true`). In fight.gd._resolve_player_swing(), if is_riposte_attack and kind == weak, apply damage even to idle enemy, add +2 momentum to player, and reset riposte_window_ticks = 0. Visually: riposte window creates a distinct blocking pose — use blocking_active cell (4) but with a gold/yellow tint (Color(1.0, 1.0, 0.3, 1.0)) for the final 4 ticks to show 'ready to riposte'. Riposte attack swing uses attacking_weak cell (1) as normal.
features.spriteWorkaround: Riposte-ready window uses blocking_active cell (4) with a gold tint applied conditionally in player._update_sprite() (check riposte_window_ticks > 0 and modulate *= Color(1.0, 1.0, 0.3)). No sprite change needed, just tint.
features.risks: Riposte adds UI complexity; players must learn the gold tint = ready state. If the window (4 ticks ≈ 0.25 sec) is too short, riposte becomes a 'gotcha' dead mechanic. Recommend 6 ticks (0.375 sec) for first pass. Damage (15 HP even on idle) is strong; may need to nerf to 12 or gate it only to weak hits that would normally miss. Testing will reveal if this breaks idle-block strategy.

result.cohesionRationale: These four features form a tight progression loop: (1) Momentum Meter creates a resource tension — hits build it, misses or enemy hits burn it, decay forces engagement. (2) Cleave turns momentum into a high-stakes decision: spend big for massive damage, but face the risk of whiffing on a prediction. (3) Execute Threshold rewards aggressive play when the kill is near, removing hesitation penalties. (4) Riposte Window feeds momentum back into the system after defense, making blocking an active offensive tool rather than pure survival. Together, they shift the game from "read the telegraph" into "manage resources while reading telegraphs" — a second layer of depth that rewards both prediction and skillful moment-to-moment play. Momentum decay prevents runaway scaling; all four features stay tied to the 16-tick tick clock; no new physics or collision logic needed.
result.feasibilityConcerns: 1. Momentum Meter is solid and low-risk (HUD bar, decay counter, no FSM changes). 2. Cleave adds an FSM state + input gate (cost check), which is straightforward but requires careful tuning — windup prediction vs. enemy attack timing is a design knot. 3. Execute Threshold is nearly free (conditional damage scaling + tint pulse), but modulate conflicts with hit flash need careful resolution. 4. Riposte is the riskiest: sub-state tracking, flag-passing between fight.gd/player.gd, and the riposte window tint is a fourth visual state (after idle, active, passive) — adds hidden complexity. If time is tight, Momentum + Cleave + Execute are a solid 3-feature package; Riposte is the polish layer. Overall feasibility is high because none of these require new physics, collision, or sprite art — all are state machine + balance tuning.
result.topPick: **Momentum Meter + Execute Threshold** (if only two features ship). Momentum Meter is the foundation — it's low-effort, high-visibility, and immediately changes the feel of combat (hits feel rewarding, misses feel costly). Execute Threshold is nearly free implementation-wise (one conditional check + a tint pulse) but transforms the endgame from "grind the last 25 HP" into "finish strong." Together, they add dramatic upside (momentum payoff + execute damage spike) with clear visual/mechanical feedback. Riposte and Cleave are excellent depth-adders but require more testing and tuning; these two can ship first and validate the momentum concept before layering complexity.

result.theme: Each defeated enemy spawns a slightly tougher variant; your performance tier (S/A/B/C) unlocks modifiers that escalate difficulty and score multiplicatively, making every run feel distinct.

features.name: Wave System with Enemy Scaling
features.pitch: After the first enemy dies, a new (slightly tougher) enemy_basic spawns immediately. Each wave scales threat/recovery timings and attack probabilities from balance.json, encouraging players to adapt their rhythm or lose momentum.
features.mechanics:
On enemy death (hp <= 0), instead of showing VYHRA, decrement a global wave number and spawn a fresh enemy. In fight._on_tick(), before the state-expired check, poll a new float via Balance.wave_scale_factor(wave_num) that linearly increases threat timings and strong probability:

- Wave 1 (first enemy): scale = 1.0 (baseline)
- Wave 2: scale = 1.1 → threat_weak = 8 * 1.1 = ~9 ticks, strong_probability = 15% * 1.1 ≈ 16.5%
- Wave 3: scale = 1.2 → threat_weak = 9.6 ticks, etc.
- Scales via a new balance.json key `wave_scale_per_enemy: 0.1` (multiplicative per wave)

Enemy is re-instantiated fresh (hp and fatigue caps), but _threat_weak_ticks() and _threat_strong_ticks() read a wave multiplier. No new sprite cells needed — same enemy_basic sheet.
features.spriteWorkaround: Same enemy_basic sprite cells; scaling is purely timing-based, no new visuals. Could optionally modulate enemy color (reddish tint) per wave for visual feedback, but not required.
features.risks: Scaling can get out of hand — threat window becomes 4 ticks if unchecked. Cap scaling in balance.json (e.g. max_wave_scale: 2.0). Also: if player dies in future, must decide whether wave persists or resets (out of scope v1 since player is invincible).

features.name: Combo Counter & Rank Tiers
features.pitch: Every hit builds a combo (display at top center). Hitting enemy during its recovery/exhausted phase extends the combo; taking a hit (regen on player miss) or missing an opening resets it to 0. Reach 5+ hits → A rank, 10+ → S rank. Rank is visual feedback and future score multiplier.
features.mechanics:
Introduce a new Combatant property combo_count (int) and a new HUD field combo_rank ("None"/"C"/"B"/"A"/"S"). In fight._resolve_player_swing(), if the swing lands (hp damage applied), increment player.combo_count += 1. If enemy regen fires (player miss → enemy.heal_hp), set player.combo_count = 0.

Combo rank tiers (hardcoded, no JSON needed yet — could move to balance later):
- 0 hits: None
- 1-4 hits: C rank
- 5-9 hits: B rank
- 10-14 hits: A rank
- 15+: S rank

Display combo_count + rank at top center of HUD (HUD.gd, in _on_canvas_draw, new y-position ≈ 80). Combo visual: large font color shifts per tier (C=yellow, B=cyan, A=gold, S=red/glow).

On wave restart (new enemy spawned), reset combo_count = 0.
features.spriteWorkaround: Purely UI-based. Color tints in HUD are hardcoded const Color tuples; no new sprites.
features.risks: None if carefully scoped: combo is read-only from player perspective (no gameplay rule changes, pure feedback layer). Ensure reset is synchronized with wave spawn.

features.name: Score Multiplier per Rank + Duel Timer Bonus
features.pitch: Each wave, calculate a base score = (enemy_max_hp - final_hp) + (time_to_kill_in_seconds * bonus_per_sec). Rank multiplier applies: S=2.5x, A=2.0x, B=1.5x, C=1.0x. Top-left HUD shows running total. Breaking personal best triggers a visual pulse.
features.mechanics:
Introduce a global game session state in fight.gd:
- total_score (int)
- waves_cleared (int)
- personal_best_score (int, persisted locally in future; for now just in-session)
- wave_start_tick (int, recorded when enemy spawns; used to compute kill time)

After each kill, in fight._on_tick():
1. Compute enemy_damage_dealt = (Balance.hp_max - enemy.hp) [how much we didn't let regen]
2. Compute time_to_kill_ticks = current_tick - wave_start_tick
3. Compute time_bonus_score = (time_to_kill_ticks / 16) * Balance.score_bonus_per_second [convert ticks to seconds]
4. Compute wave_score = enemy_damage_dealt + time_bonus_score
5. Compute rank_multiplier based on player.combo_rank at end of wave
6. final_wave_score = wave_score * rank_multiplier
7. total_score += final_wave_score
8. If total_score > personal_best_score: personal_best_score = total_score; queue a visual pulse (brief white flash on score text or a floating "NEW BEST" sprite)

Display in HUD top-left, e.g.:
  SCORE: 4250 [S rank]
  BEST: 6180
  WAVE: 3

Add to balance.json: `score_bonus_per_second: 10` (tunable).
features.spriteWorkaround: UI-only. Visual pulse on new best is a modulate flash or brief color change on the score text — no new sprites.
features.risks: Score math can overflow int if waves scale too high. Use clampi(total_score, 0, 999999). Time bonus could incentivize rushing and dying (out of scope here but noted for future loss conditions).

features.name: Difficulty Mutator (Aggressive Mode Toggle)
features.pitch: A cheat/unlockable modifier toggled at the start of a run (before first wave). AGGRESSIVE: enemy idle_min decreases by 50%, strong_probability increases 3x. Forces tighter decision-making, rewards mastery, and creates a high-score category ("Aggressive run").
features.mechanics:
Add a boolean flag run_is_aggressive (default false) to fight.gd. In the win/restart overlay (when show_win is true), before Space is pressed, allow player to press 'M' to toggle mutator text "AGGRESSIVE MODE: ON/OFF". Only applies after restart.

When _entering_ the first enemy of a run, apply the mutator via Balance overrides (or direct code):
- If run_is_aggressive:
  - idle_min_ticks *= 0.5 → idle is 6-24 ticks instead of 12-48, enemy attacks twice as often
  - strong_probability *= 3.0, clamped to 1.0 → 45% strong attacks instead of 15%
  - threat_strong_ticks -= 4 → strong winds up in 8 ticks, not 12

Score display includes an "AGGRESSIVE" tag when active (red text in HUD), and aggressive runs are mentally distinct (future: could store separate leaderboard).

No balance.json keys needed; mutator timings are hardcoded const floats in fight.gd.
features.spriteWorkaround: UI-only: mutator toggle text and label in HUD during win/restart state. No sprite changes.
features.risks: Easy to break game balance if multipliers are too extreme (45% strong + 6-tick threat → enemy becomes unpredictably fast). Tune numbers via playtesting before shipping. Also: must respect player invincibility — aggressive should NOT make waves unwinnable, just harder.

result.cohesionRationale: These four features form a cohesive progression loop: (1) Waves force the player to adapt their rhythm each round and stay engaged (2) Combo counter incentivizes reading enemy recovery windows cleanly, rewarding skilled play immediately (3) Score multiplier per rank + timer bonus makes every run's performance quantifiable, creating a feedback loop where better ranks = higher scores (4) Aggressive mutator lets mastery players opt into harder challenges and compete on a separate leaderboard. Together, they transform a one-off fight into a roguelike-lite run where players want to \"one-more-run\" to beat their score or chase S ranks on every wave. No conflicts: all are toggle-able, none break existing rules or sprite architecture."
result.feasibilityConcerns: Wave scaling (feature 1) requires modifying enemy_basic.gd's tunable accessors to read a per-wave multiplier — this is clean but adds a small state dependency (current wave number must flow to enemy.gd). Combo rank (feature 2) is pure additive UI, safest change. Score multiplier (feature 3) involves tick-counting for time bonus, which is straightforward but requires careful initialization per wave (wave_start_tick). Aggressive mode (feature 4) is UI-only but requires brief input-handling in win state (detecting 'M' key). All four avoid sprite art, physics, and hard architecture changes. The main risk is tuning: if wave scaling gets out of hand (threat window shrinks to 2 ticks), the game becomes unplayable. Mitigation: cap scaling in balance.json. Secondary risk: score overflow, but clampi() solves it. Overall, all four are low-risk given careful balance tuning."
result.topPick: **Combo Counter & Rank Tiers (Feature 2)** — if only ONE ships, this is it. Why: It's the smallest change (one int on Combatant, one HUD draw call, two resolve-time checks) but has outsized impact on game feel. Right now, when you land a hit, there's no feedback beyond health dropping. Combo counter makes EVERY hit feel rewarding, encouraging players to chain hits and read recovery windows more carefully. It also creates a natural „rank" vocabulary (S/A/B/C) that future features (leaderboards, cutscenes, etc.) can hang onto. The juice factor is highest because it changes how players *experience* moment-to-moment gameplay without changing any rules. It's the fastest to implement (S effort) and riskiest zero-cost win."

summary: Panel of design lenses proposes interesting new features for the Glamdring reaction-combat game, each self-checked for feasibility against the real codebase

phases.title: Ideate
phases.detail: one agent per design lens, each reads the repo and proposes a feature package
